subject: Statistics
title: Herd Immunity by the Numbers
description: A tiny lesson with a big payoff: Herd Immunity by the Numbers.

hook.text: Measles needs 95% vaccination to stop spreading. Polio needs 80%. Why the difference?

buildup.text: Herd immunity occurs when enough people are immune that the disease can't find new hosts.

discovery.text: The threshold depends on R0 — the basic reproduction number. Higher R0 means a higher threshold is needed.

twist.text: Measles has R0 ≈ 15. Each case infects 15 others. That's why even small vaccination gaps cause outbreaks.

climax.text: Herd immunity protects those who can't be vaccinated — infants, immunocompromised, and the elderly.

punchline.text: Protect enough people and the virus runs out of targets.

quiz.question: What determines the herd immunity threshold for a disease?
options: The basic reproduction number (R0) — more contagious diseases need higher vaccination rates
